author: CC (from Akien's observation)
relates_to: node_classes/ — and intentions-other/usage-widget-hook.json, the first host-seam
text:
AKIEN'S OBSERVATION (2026-07-22, from 'yank the UU debris out of ~/bin'): 'that right there is work that doesn't result in code, has an intention, but that intention is really complete when it's done. We appear to have a new class of thing! Maintenance tasks will have intentions and whys and proofs; they become workflows. But when the workflow is complete the intention does not need to remain. And the intention might then be really done... or if it's a recurrent maintenance activity, the completed workflow would go to some logging depot (flaggable for 30-day or permanent storage), and the future maintenance intention would just get a new workflow instance (ticket) the next time its trigger rolled around.'

CC'S READ, offered for adjudication, NOT settled: the thing Akien found is real, but it may be an AXIS rather than a fifth node_class. A node_class answers two questions — where does it berth, and what gate proves it. Maintenance doesn't answer either consistently: yanking stale symlinks is a host-seam (the implementation is the machine; it already gets an apply recipe and a re-runnable verify), while pruning stale tickets or paying off the members_so_far honesty debt is repo work. Maintenance cuts ACROSS the classes rather than sitting beside them, which is the signature of an axis.

The axis it seems to be: WHAT BECOMES OF THE TICKET WHEN IT RESOLVES. Two dispositions. (1) FREEZE — the voyage becomes the component's `history`; it is PROVENANCE, and the code cannot be understood without it. (2) DEPOT — the voyage becomes a RECEIPT; nothing downstream needs to read 'we removed 12 dangling symlinks on 2026-07-22' to understand the system, so it goes to a log with a retention flag. Every ticket already has to answer this; we only noticed because until now every ticket answered FREEZE.

AND THE RETENTION FLAG MAY NOT NEED INVENTING. The three-roots rule already decides it: 'if losing it loses knowledge, it's commons.' A permanent-flagged maintenance record is knowledge -> CairnCommons. A 30-day receipt loses nothing -> ~/.cairn/ (instance-space, where logs already live by law). So the flag is the commons test applied per-record, and the depot is not one new store but the existing partition doing its job. Guess, to be adjusted (Law 3).

THE ONE PLACE CC WOULD PUSH BACK: 'the intention does not need to remain.' MAP and CLAUDE.md both hold that intentions are elaborated or superseded but NEVER closed, and that nothing is terminal. A cleaner reading that keeps that intact: the INTENTION is standing ('no dead-predecessor debris on PATH'), the TICKET is the disposable instance, and a one-shot is just a standing intention whose trigger will never fire again — UU stopped being a live source of debris, so the intention survives with an empty member set. That collapses Akien's two cases (really-done vs recurrent) into one mechanism instead of two, and it does not require an exception to nothing-is-terminal. If instead the intention genuinely evaporates, that IS an exception to a stated principle and should be recorded as one, loudly, rather than absorbed quietly.

WHY IT MATTERS BEYOND FILING: the recurrence machinery already exists and is green. A maintenance intention with a horizon IS a trigger (any predicate, evaluated where its data is owned — base/callback.py), and 'spawn a ticket when it fires' is a callback. So recurrent maintenance is likely wiring, not construction — which would make it, alongside the repo scanner, a candidate first member of `operational-driver` (the node class defined 2026-07-18 that still has zero members).

STATUS: un-cast. Akien to rule on axis-vs-class before this goes near /sorted.
